title: TLS, SSE-KMS, Bucket Keys, and Key Operations · Spiral Review
summary: A delayed handoff reconstructs tls, sse-kms, bucket keys, and key operations from browser, Node, TypeScript, AWS SDK v3, S3, CloudFront, security, billing, and stakeholder evidence and challenges one compile-time misconception. Planned artifact: Extend the evidence record for encryption and key-lifecycle contract after a delayed changed case. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: In a strict Node 24 and TypeScript 7 service, separate TLS in transit, S3 server-side encryption, KMS envelope encryption, and application client-side encryption by threat and feature tradeoff.
In a strict Node 24 and TypeScript 7 service, choose SSE-S3, SSE-KMS, dual-layer KMS where required, or client encryption from audit, tenancy, performance, cost, and service integration needs.
In a strict Node 24 and TypeScript 7 service, constrain IAM and KMS key policy, grants, service principal, encryption context, region, rotation, disable, deletion, and break-glass ownership.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: S3, CloudFront, and TypeScript Transport At Rest Client · changed case · Review Transport At Rest C…
steps.instruction:
review case — A review TypeScript file-delivery team handles case 4397 during a Node stream backpressure incident. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to separate TLS, server encryption, KMS policy, context, and rotation evidence; keep every external value unknown until a runtime validator accepts it; then change region, retrieval, or transfer cost and defend the revised budget action. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. Node and AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, PostgreSQL, credentials, host state, handle and stream checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.

Which action demonstrates Transport At Rest Client without dropping earlier contracts?

At S3, CloudFront, and TypeScript Separate evidence from confidence · Review Key Rotation Revocation D…, trace this concrete input and predict runtime validation, authorization, custody, integrity, range, stream, cache, retry, cost, cleanup, and recovery evidence before reviewing it: A review TypeScript file-delivery team handles case 5734 during a browser upload compatibility rollout. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to separate TLS, server encryption, KMS policy, context, and rotation evidence; do not treat TypeScript types, a resolved promise, CORS, ETag, or an emulator as stronger proof than it is; then abort during a stream or multipart promise and prove every task and part reaches a terminal state. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. Node and AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, PostgreSQL, credentials, host state, handle and stream checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: In a strict Node 24 and TypeScript 7 service, prove new writes, old reads, role rotation, key disable recovery, compromised-session revocation limits, and residual cached or signed access.

For S3, CloudFront, and TypeScript Separate evidence from confidence · Review Key Rotation Revocation D…, challenge the documented misconception with runtime, file, object, policy, transfer, integrity, cache, fault, cost, restore, and controlled AWS transfer evidence: Rotating a KMS key immediately re-encrypts every existing object and revokes every URL. Type annotations, generated SDK shapes, and resolved promises do not strengthen that runtime claim.

stimulus.title: Key Rotation Revocation Drill case record
stimulus.caption: Three excerpts from a learner evidence packet; only one connects a claim to a reproducible check.

lines.label: assumption
lines.text: A review TypeScript file-delivery team handles case 5734 during a browser upload compatibility rollout. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to separate TLS, server encryption, KMS policy, context, and rotation evidence; do not treat TypeScript types, a resolved promise, CORS, ETag, or an emulator as stronger proof than it is; then abort during a stream or multipart promise and prove every task and part reaches a terminal state. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. Node and AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, PostgreSQL, credentials, host state, handle and stream checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: In a strict Node 24 and TypeScript 7 service, prove new writes, old reads, role rotation, key disable recovery, compromised-session revocation limits, and residual cached or signed access.

lines.label: risk
lines.text: Rotating a KMS key immediately re-encrypts every existing object and revokes every URL. Type annotations, generated SDK shapes, and resolved promises do not strengthen that runtime claim.

lines.label: evidence
lines.text: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: In a strict Node 24 and TypeScript 7 service, prove new writes, old reads, role rotation, key disable recovery, compromised-session revocation limits, and residual cached or signed access. Artifact under review: encryption and key-lifecycle contract.

options.text: For separate evidence from confidence, apply Key Rotation Revocation Drill without its stated preconditions, omit the encryption and key-lifecycle contract trace and counterexample, and treat one plausible output as complete proof. This does not satisfy Key Rotation Revocation Drill for encryption and key-lifecycle contract.
The record names the assumption, applies Key Rotation Revocation Drill, and reports a changed-case check tied to the stakeholder decision.

The Key Rotation Revocation Drill record says encryption and key-lifecycle contract looks right but supplies no governing condition, observation, changed case, or counterexample. That claim fails the separate evidence from confidence evidence boundary for Key Rotation Revocation Drill.

steps.title: S3, CloudFront, and TypeScript Record what required effort · Review Key Rotation Revocation Drill
steps.instruction:
Write the review evidence note for Key Rotation Revocation Drill: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — A review TypeScript file-delivery team handles case 4115 during a Node stream backpressure incident. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to separate TLS, server encryption, KMS policy, context, and rotation evidence; separate deterministic browser evidence from controlled Node and AWS transfer gates; then change object version, signed header, or cache dimension and reject stale authority. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. Node and AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, PostgreSQL, credentials, host state, handle and stream checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: In a strict Node 24 and TypeScript 7 service, prove new writes, old reads, role rotation, key disable recovery, compromised-session revocation limits, and residual cached or signed access.

Evidence target: S3, CloudFront, and TypeScript Record what required effort · Review Key Rotation Revocation Drill.
steps.why: Retrieving Key Rotation Revocation Drill through the concrete encryption and key-lifecycle contract case exposes causal gaps that recognition alone can hide. For S3, CloudFront, and TypeScript Record what required effort · Review Key Rotation Revocation Drill, keep Key Rotation Revocation Drill connected to executable TypeScript storage and delivery decision evidence.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: In a strict Node 24 and TypeScript 7 service, prove new writes, old reads, role rotation, key disable recovery, compromised-session revocation limits, and residual cached or signed access.
